title: Florentines
subtitle: Italian lace biscuits with toasted almonds and mixed peel, chocolate-backed
excerpt: Thin, lacey Italian biscuits made from a mixture of butter, sugar, cream, and honey with flaked almonds, mixed peel, and a little flour. The mixture is spooned in small circles onto a lined tray and baked until golden and set. Once cool, the flat side of each biscuit is coated in dark chocolate, combed with a fork to create a wavy pattern, and left to set. The edges are typically irregular; that is the nature of a florentine.
sourceNotes: Florentines are associated with both Italian and French patisserie traditions; their name suggests a Florentine origin though they are made throughout Italy and France. The combination of caramelised sugar, cream, almonds, and candied peel with a chocolate backing is documented in French patisserie texts from the nineteenth century. Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903, Project Gutenberg), describes similar petit four preparations. The precise recipe as currently known is consistent across Italian and French home-baking literature of the early twentieth century.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Store in an airtight tin interleaved with baking paper for up to 1 week. Chocolate-coated side down keeps them neater.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 160°C. Bake for 10 to 14 minutes until deep golden all over. Watch carefully; the high sugar content means they go from golden to burnt very quickly.
baking.decoratingTechnique: chocolate backing combed with a fork

recipeTools.notes: two baking trays, lined with baking paper

glossaryTerms.definition: Florentines spread significantly during baking as the sugar and butter melt and flow. Controlling spread means using small amounts of mixture per biscuit (a heaped teaspoon), spacing them well apart on the tray (at least 6 cm), and chilling the mixture for 15 minutes if it seems too liquid to hold its initial shape. As the mixture heats, it first spreads then sets as the sugar caramelises. If florentines spread into a single mass, they can sometimes be trimmed back to circles with a cookie cutter while still hot and pliable.

content.text: The main challenge with florentines is
: keep the portions small and well-spaced. Work in batches of 6 to 8 per tray so you have time to trim any that merge. They must be fully cooled and firm before the chocolate goes on.

Heat the oven to 180°C conventional (160°C fan). Line two trays with baking paper. Melt the butter, sugar, cream, and golden syrup together in a small saucepan over gentle heat, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Remove from the heat and stir in the flour, flaked almonds, and mixed peel. Leave to cool for 5 minutes.

Drop heaped teaspoon-sized portions onto the trays, spacing them at least 6 cm apart (they will spread). Bake for 10 to 14 minutes until deep golden all over. While still hot and flexible (about 30 seconds out of the oven), use a round cutter or a knife to nudge any irregular edges back to a rough circle. Leave to cool completely on the trays; they firm up as they cool.

Melt the dark chocolate in a bowl set over a pan of barely simmering water. Flip the cold florentines upside down. Spread a layer of melted chocolate on the flat underside of each. Draw a fork through the chocolate in a wavy pattern. Set on baking paper chocolate-side up until the chocolate is completely firm.
